title: Bridge Essay: Moral conviction interacts with metacognitive ability in modulating neural activity during sociopolitical decision-making.
concept: Metacognition as the 'diagnostic' capacity — science of knowing what you know, parallel to esoteric self-examination.

body.text: This bridge paper connects the esoteric/philosophical concept of **Dashboard Diagnosis** with mechanistic science from PubMed/MEDLINE.
**Bridge rationale:** Metacognition as the 'diagnostic' capacity — science of knowing what you know, parallel to esoteric self-examination.

**Moral conviction interacts with metacognitive ability in modulating neural activity during sociopolitical decision-making.** - Cognitive, affective & behavioral neuroscience (2025). DOI: 10.3758/s13415-024-01243-3. PMID: 39702726. The extent to which a belief is rooted in one's sense of morality has significant societal implications. While moral conviction can inspire positive collective action, it can also prompt dogmatism, intolerance, and societal divisions. Research in social psychology has documented the functional characteristics of moral conviction and shows that poor metacognition exacerbates its negative outcomes.
